funders.blurb: The AI Risk Mitigation (ARM) Fund, a project of Effective Ventures Foundation spun out of the Long-Term Future Fund in December 2023, makes grants to reduce catastrophic AI risks through technical alignment research, AI policy work, and programs that recruit new researchers into AI safety.
funders.thesis: Reduce catastrophic risks from advanced AI through grants towards technical research, policy, and training programs for new researchers — AI companies alone are not enough, with strikingly few researchers working on key safety problems outside major industry labs.

apply.notes: Currently doing proactive grantmaking only; no open application portal as of mid-2026. Prospective grantees can email info@airiskfund.com to express interest. Site states no set date for open applications.

sources.title: AI Risk Mitigation Fund — official site
AI Risk Mitigation Fund — About (team page)
AI Risk Mitigation Fund — Grants

Rewrote to track the site's own language more closely, preserving the verified 'AI companies alone are not enough' framing (the site has a section with that exact title).

funders.blurb: Longview Philanthropy is an EA-aligned nonprofit grantmaker that has directed over $204 million toward AI risk reduction as of June 2026, funding technical safety research, policy development, and field-building through its public Emerging Challenges Fund and private Frontier AI Fund.
funders.thesis: Private markets systematically underinvest in managing AI risks because profits concentrate among a few companies with little incentive to prioritize societal interests over short-term gains; philanthropy can fill this gap by advancing cutting-edge technical research on alignment and interpretability, shaping governance frameworks, and building the field of AI safety.

apply.notes: Emerging Challenges Fund is a public grantmaking program; the private Frontier AI Fund is for donors giving over $100,000 and is invite-access only.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence - Longview Philanthropy
AI Safety and Alignment Grant Program (2026) | Granted AI

funders.blurb: Founders Pledge's Global Catastrophic Risks Fund makes philanthropic grants to high-impact organizations addressing AI safety policy, governance, and resilience — alongside biosecurity and nuclear risk — prioritizing hits-based policy advocacy and field-building in underinvested areas.
funders.thesis: "Reduce the probability of large-scale catastrophic events; mitigate the potential negative impacts of these events if they occur; improve the ability to anticipate new and emerging risks on the horizon." The fund addresses AI risks through diplomatic confidence-building measures, government detection and response capacity, and resilience-building interventions it considers relatively neglected compared to technical alignment work.

apply.notes: Fund primarily identifies grantees through its own research and expert networks; external expressions of interest can be submitted via the requests-for-funding page, though that page's stated criteria skew toward development programs. No formal rounds or deadlines.

sources.title: Global Catastrophic Risks Fund — Founders Pledge

Building global resilience to AI risks — Founders Pledge

funders.blurb: Halcyon Futures is a nonprofit incubator and early-stage fund that backs founders in AI safety, biosecurity, and critical cybersecurity through grants up to $1M, venture investments, and career transition support.
funders.thesis: The single biggest bottleneck to addressing these risks is exceptional founders and leaders.

apply.notes: Apply via founders page or hello@halcyonfutures.org; no formal deadline; seeks seasoned founders, senior operators, technical researchers, and policy/defense veterans

sources.title: Halcyon Futures – Official Site

funders.blurb: Early-stage venture fund that writes $100K SAFEs into startups building tools for AI safety, security, interpretability, and responsible deployment; founded April 2025 by former Y Combinator president Geoff Ralston.
funders.thesis: "Help exceptional founders build products that support a future where AI is both powerful and aligned with human interests" — a bet on a future that is both accelerated and aligned, not a reaction to hype or fear.

apply.notes: Applications accepted on an ongoing basis via the Request Funding form on saif.vc; no fixed deadline.

_verifyChanges: Blurb: changed 'equity investments (SAFE notes)' to 'SAFEs'. SAFEs (Simple Agreements for Future Equity) are not notes — notes are debt instruments that accrue interest. The TechCrunch source describes these as '$100,000 checks as a SAFE.' Also removed 'equity investments' framing since SAFEs are pre-equity convertible instruments, not direct equity.

People: Michael Ralston (Partner) is publicly listed on https://www.saif.vc/team alongside Geoff and Nick Ralston, but omitted from the record. Noted here; the 2-person field maximum prevents adding him without displacing a confirmed entry, so the two existing entries (both verified) are retained as-is.

funders.blurb: Craig Newmark Philanthropies is the personal philanthropy of craigslist founder Craig Newmark, which has committed $100 million to a Cyber Civil Defense initiative funding cybersecurity workforce development, tools development, and critical infrastructure protection.
funders.thesis: Cybersecurity is a modern patriotic duty — a "whole-of-society" cyber civil defense modeled on historical civil defense, where citizens, nonprofits, and institutions actively participate to protect critical infrastructure, power grids, and connected devices from nation-state and criminal cyber threats.

apply.notes: Accepts unsolicited proposals via online form with PDF attachment; brief proposals preferred; proposals supporting military families or veterans should apply through the Bob Woodruff Foundation portal instead

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: Stated $100M commitment to Cyber Civil Defense network launched in 2022

funders.blurb: DARPA's Information Innovation Office (I2O) funds high-risk AI security and cybersecurity R&D through office-wide BAAs covering trustworthy AI, resilient software, and offensive/defensive cyber capabilities, and previously ran the AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) prize competition ($29.5M total prizes, concluded Aug 2025).
funders.thesis: AI that is significantly more reliable and predictable in high-stakes settings, understandable to its operators, and secure in contested environments — achieved through AI interpretability, verifiable human control, and adversarial robustness research.

apply.notes: I2O office-wide BAA (HR001126S0001): abstracts on rolling basis until Nov 1 2026, full proposals until Nov 30 2026; covers trustworthy/ethically-aligned AI, resilient secure software, and offensive/defensive cybersecurity. AI Cyber Challenge competition concluded at DEF CON Aug 2025; AI Forge RFI deadline passed Jun 22 2026 with summer 2026 forum launch pending.

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: Total prize pool committed for AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) two-year competition (2023–2025), including $7M reserved for small businesses

_verifyChanges: Blurb updated: AIxCC is a concluded program (finals at DEF CON Aug 2025, prizes awarded per darpa.mil/news/2025/aixcc-results). Reworded to past tense for AIxCC and foregrounded the active BAA as the current funding mechanism.

domainTags: stripped 'evals'. No source connects DARPA I2O to model-safety evaluations; AIxCC was a prize competition, not an evals framework in the AI-safety sense. 'agent-security' and 'interpretability' remain, both directly supported by AI Forge article and AIxCC program page.

SAM.gov (the primary source) returned no usable content via WebFetch. This is the main residual weakness.
Thesis retained verbatim: language confirmed nearly word-for-word from the AI Forge DARPA press release (June 1 2026). Factual, no marketing superlatives.

funders.blurb: IARPA funds competitive multi-year R&D programs for the U.S. Intelligence Community on AI security, including trojan/backdoor detection in deployed models (TrojAI, completed 2025) and LLM threat quantification and mitigation (BENGAL, active).
funders.thesis: "To defend AI systems from intentional, malicious attacks, known as Trojans, by conducting research and developing technology to detect these attacks in a completed AI system" and "to understand the landscape of LLM threats and vulnerabilities, with the goal of developing novel technologies to analyze and address these shortcomings, enabling the Intelligence Community to safely harness LLM technology across a broad range of critical applications."

apply.notes: IARPA funds research via competitive Broad Agency Announcements (BAAs) posted on iarpa.gov and sam.gov. TrojAI BAA (2019) and BENGAL BAA (deadline Jan 29, 2024) are both closed; performer teams have been selected. Monitor open-baas page for new AI-security solicitations.

IARPA Advances BENGAL Program (MeriTalk)

_verifyChanges: Stripped 'interpretability' from domainTags: IARPA's TrojAI and BENGAL programs focus on trojan/backdoor detection and LLM threat analysis (security and evals), not mechanistic interpretability. No IARPA source describes interpretability as a program goal; applying 'prefer stripping when in doubt' rule.

Added MeriTalk BENGAL source (https://www.meritalk.com/articles/iarpa-advances-bengal-program/) to sources: the record's blurb prominently features BENGAL but the original source list had no BENGAL-specific citation. MeriTalk article confirms performer selection in 2024 and BENGAL's active status.
Added Tim McKinnon's LinkedIn profileUrl (https://www.linkedin.com/in/timothy-mckinnon/) verified via web search showing his public LinkedIn profile confirms his IARPA/BENGAL role.

All other claims verified: Kristopher Reese confirmed as TrojAI Program Manager via AFCEA article and IARPA program managers page. Tim McKinnon confirmed as BENGAL Program Manager. TrojAI thesis quote confirmed from IARPA official sources. BENGAL thesis quote consistent with multiple secondary sources. annualFieldGivingUSD null is correct (no public funding figure). Apply mode 'rounds' / deadline null is accurate — both cited BAAs closed, no current AI-security solicitation open. BENGAL confirmed active (kicked off May 2026 with six prime performers).

funders.blurb: NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) — successor to the U.S. AI Safety Institute — conducts voluntary evaluations of AI capabilities for national security risks and develops security guidelines and standards for AI on behalf of the U.S. government.
funders.thesis: CAISI serves as "industry's primary point of contact within the U.S. government to facilitate testing and collaborative research related to harnessing and securing the potential of commercial AI systems," focusing on evaluations covering cybersecurity, biosecurity, and chemical weapons risks.

apply.notes: The NIST AI Consortium (the membership body through which CAISI conducts collaborative research) accepts letters of interest from all organizations that can contribute expertise, products, data, or models — open on an ongoing basis with biannual review periods; first review began June 2026. Participants enter a CRADA with NIST. No funding is provided — this is a collaborative research arrangement, not a grant program. CAISI does not operate a public grant program.

sources.title: Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) | NIST
What Will It Cost for the US to Be Ready for the Next Big AI Breakthrough? | IFP
NIST Expands AI Consortium's Scope, Calls for New Members | NIST

_verifyChanges: apply.mode changed from 'invite-only' to 'rolling': the apply URL page (updated 2026-06-24) explicitly states participation is open to all interested organizations on an ongoing basis, selected first-come first-served — directly contradicting 'invite-only'.

apply.notes rewritten: removed unconfirmed specific lab names (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, xAI) — no fetched source confirms these as current CAISI pre-deployment evaluation partners; CAISI page cited OpenMined and GSA as recent partners instead. blurb updated: removed 'in June 2025' (rename date not confirmed by any fetched NIST page; a dedicated URL returned 404) and changed 'pre-deployment safety evaluations of frontier AI models with major labs' to language closer to what the current CAISI page actually says ('voluntary evaluations of AI capabilities for national security risks'). The pre-deployment-with-labs framing was USAISI-era; no fetched source confirms CAISI continues that specific framing or those specific lab relationships.

aliases: removed 'AISI' — this abbreviation is the UK AI Safety Institute's primary designation and using it for the US organization creates ambiguity. 'US AI Safety Institute' and 'AI Safety Institute Consortium' retained as confirmed predecessor names.

funders.blurb: EPSRC (part of UKRI) co-funds the Systemic AI Safety Grants Programme (£8.5m total commitment) with Innovate UK, with the AI Safety Institute managing applications; Phase 1 supported approximately 20 projects at up to £200k each on systemic risks from AI in critical infrastructure and societal systems.
funders.thesis: Develop an initial understanding of systemic AI safety risks and identify promising mitigations that can be promoted in future programme phases; the programme targets risks that arise at a systems level — in critical infrastructure and societal systems — rather than from individual model capabilities alone.

apply.notes: AI safety-specific calls run as competitive rounds co-administered with AISI. Phase 1 of the Systemic AI Safety Grants Programme closed November 2024; ~20 projects funded at up to £200k each. Future rounds expected but none announced as of July 2026. Standard EPSRC research grants accept applications on a rolling basis year-round.

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: £8.5m total programme commitment for Systemic AI Safety Grants Programme (EPSRC + Innovate UK), converted at 1.27 USD/GBP

funders.blurb: Horizon Europe is the EU's main R&I funding programme (2021–2027, €93.5B total) that issues competitive calls for AI security and trustworthy AI research; the dedicated SecureAI call (HORIZON-CL3-2026-02-CS-ECCC-02, €21.2M) specifically targets hardening AI systems against adversarial attacks, backdoor injections, and data poisoning in compliance with the EU AI Act.
funders.thesis: "Strengthen the resilience of AI systems and algorithms against various threats and attacks" — proposals should develop real-time anomaly detection, mitigation techniques to defend against adversarial attacks, and robust federated learning techniques, in synergies with leading efforts on AI transparency and in compliance with the AI Act.

apply.notes: Proposals submitted via the EU Funding and Tenders Portal. The SecureAI call (HORIZON-CL3-2026-02-CS-ECCC-02) closes 15 September 2026 at 17:00 CEST. Work programmes are published annually; both single-stage and two-stage application processes are used depending on the call topic.

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: Budget for HORIZON-CL3-2026-02-CS-ECCC-02 (SecureAI) call in the 2026 Horizon Europe work programme; EUR 21.2M converted at ~1.09 USD/EUR

sources.title: HORIZON-CL3-2026-02-CS-ECCC SecureAI call — ECCC

_verifyChanges: Fixed timezone in apply.notes: 'CET' corrected to 'CEST'. The ECCC call page (source 1) states the deadline as '15 September 2026, 17:00 CEST'. Central Europe observes CEST (UTC+2) in September; CET (UTC+1) is the winter standard.

Stripped 'technical-alignment' from domainTags. This call funds adversarial ML robustness and AI cybersecurity defenses — it is not technical alignment research in the AI safety sense (value specification, reward modeling, etc.). The ECCC call page confirms the focus is on defending AI systems against adversarial attacks and data poisoning, with no alignment-specific scope.

Stripped 'evals' from domainTags. The call funds development of defense mechanisms and anomaly detection systems, not AI safety evaluation methodology (red-teaming, capability benchmarks, safety evals). The tag is unwarranted given the call's confirmed scope.

funders.blurb: Cybersecurity-dedicated early-stage VC ($660M across two funds) that backs AI security and agentic-security startups from seed through Series A.
funders.thesis: "The most important action we can take today is to fuel the ideas of the world's greatest cybersecurity entrepreneurs with our collective experience, network, and passion." Ballistic is dedicated to funding and incubating cybersecurity innovations exclusively, co-founding and incubating startups alongside operators who have lived the founders' challenges.

apply.notes: Pitch via info@ballisticventures.com.

funders.blurb: SYN Ventures is a cybersecurity-specialist VC that backs early-stage companies across enterprise security — with a growing cluster of AI agent security investments including Arcade (AI agent governance), CodeIntegrity (AI agent runtime control), and Helmet Security (agentic AI communications security).
funders.thesis: Focused on investing in disruptive and innovative companies in the cybersecurity, industrial security, national defense, privacy, regulatory compliance, and data governance industries.

apply.notes: Submit pitch via online form on contact page. Flagship fund leads late-stage Seed, Series A, and B rounds for cybersecurity companies with enterprise traction; Seed fund leads or co-leads Seed rounds.

funders.blurb: Israeli cybersecurity-exclusive seed VC ($800M AUM across five funds) that backs early-stage Israeli founders building category-defining security companies, with focus areas including AI-enhanced security, non-human identity security, and cloud security.
funders.thesis: "Dedicated exclusively to cybersecurity" — partners with top Israeli founders from inception, "helping build category-leading companies that define the market" through domain expertise, hands-on company building, and access to CISOs and industry leaders; backs founders who "design with resilience" for real and urgent security problems.

apply.notes: No formal application portal; founders contact via LinkedIn or website. Focuses exclusively on Israeli-founded cybersecurity companies at seed stage.

funders.blurb: Andreessen Horowitz is a major US venture capital firm that backs AI-native security startups—covering AI-powered threat detection and social engineering prevention—through direct equity investments and its Speedrun seed accelerator.
funders.thesis: "AI-native security is the platform shift of today. Just as next-generation firewalls were not simply 'firewalls++,' AI-native security requires rebuilding prevention, detection, and response so that intelligence and automation are the foundation rather than a plug-in."

apply.notes: Speedrun accelerator (up to $1M, 12-week in-person SF program) runs in cohort rounds. SR007 deadline was May 17 2026 and is now closed; next cohort TBD. Standard VC portfolio: pitch a partner directly at any time.

thesis: stripped the second clause ('technology is security, for America and her allies...') — that quote is from Anne Neuberger's guest article on a16z.com, not authored by an a16z partner; attributing it to a16z's own thesis was a misattribution.
domainTags 'open-source' removed: no source in the record or found during verification supports this tag.

domainTags 'agent-security' removed: the kill-chain article does not cover agent security (it appeared only in a sidebar link, not discussed in the piece); no other source supports it; the blurb's 'agent security vulnerabilities' phrasing was accordingly removed.
blurb revised to remove 'agent security vulnerabilities' — unsupported by the cited sources.

funders.blurb: Multi-stage VC (seed through growth) that co-led Irregular's $80M Series A in September 2025 to secure frontier AI models, and actively tracks AI security — non-human identity and AI SOC automation — as investment thesis areas within its infrastructure practice.
funders.thesis: Redpoint backs bold builders challenging industry conventions with technical depth and conviction to build enduring $1B+ companies; within its infrastructure practice it tracks non-human identity security and AI SOC automation as distinct investment thesis areas.

apply.notes: No explicit pitch submission process described on the website; homepage lists a 'Join the Redpoint network' submission form whose purpose is not specified.

sources.title: Irregular Raises $80M to Secure the Future of AI with Frontier Security Lab | Redpoint Ventures
Irregular raises $80M to secure frontier AI models | TechCrunch
Sai Senthilkumar | Redpoint Ventures

_verifyChanges: Thesis: stripped $17B NHI TAM, $28B SOC automation TAM, 80:1 machine-identity ratio, and 20-40% analyst time figures. Exhaustive fetches of every accessible Redpoint InfraRed Report URL returned none of these figures; the PDF was too large to fetch. The 80:1 ratio originates from a CyberArk/KPMG report (not Redpoint). No Redpoint-published primary source for these exact numbers could be verified. Kept the general thesis framing about non-human identity security and AI SOC automation as investment focus areas, which is supported by the Irregular investment itself.

Apply notes: removed 'Redpoint does not accept unsolicited pitches — access is typically through warm introduction from known founders or co-investors in their network.' This claim is not stated on Redpoint's website, and it is partially contradicted by a 'Join the Redpoint network' submit form visible on their homepage. Replaced with a factual description of what the page actually shows.

Domain tags: removed 'evals'. Irregular's work is adversarial red-team security testing of frontier AI models (offensive/defensive exploits, antivirus evasion, autonomous threat assessment) — not AI safety evaluation in the standard field sense. No source in the record supports 'evals' as a Redpoint investment focus. Retained 'agent-security', which is directly supported by the Irregular investment.

funders.blurb: Google DeepMind funds external AI safety research through grant programs and co-funded calls, including a $10M multi-agent AI safety funding call (2026) co-led with Schmidt Sciences, ARIA, Google.org, and the Cooperative AI Foundation, targeting technical safety research on large-scale agent ecosystems.
funders.thesis: "AI can provide extraordinary benefits, but like all transformational technology, it could have negative impacts unless it's developed and deployed responsibly." DeepMind's safety strategy is to "anticipate and evaluate our systems against a broad spectrum of AI-related risks, taking a holistic approach to responsibility, safety and security," including funding distributed global research to identify emergent risks in multi-agent systems before they scale.

apply.notes: Active funding round: $10M multi-agent AI safety call (Tier 1 up to $300K, Tier 2 $300K–$1M), co-funded with Schmidt Sciences, ARIA, Google.org, and Cooperative AI Foundation. Open to universities, nonprofits, and international institutions; for-profits cannot lead. Deadline August 8 2026 (AoE); awardees announced Autumn 2026.

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: Up to $10M committed to the Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World funding call, co-funded with Schmidt Sciences, ARIA, Google.org, and Cooperative AI Foundation — announced June 11 2026

sources.title: Google DeepMind and partners announce multi-agent safety research funding call
Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World — Cooperative AI Foundation grants page
Responsibility & Safety — Google DeepMind

_verifyChanges: Blurb: added Google.org to the co-funders list — the DeepMind blog and Cooperative AI Foundation page both confirm five co-funders (Google DeepMind, Schmidt Sciences, ARIA, Google.org, Cooperative AI Foundation); the original blurb omitted Google.org.

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: changed 'Total committed to' to 'Up to $10M committed to' — the source says 'up to $10M', a ceiling figure not a definitive disbursement pledge. Also added Cooperative AI Foundation to the co-funder list in the method text (it was missing).
apply.notes: updated co-funders to include Google.org (consistent with blurb and basis corrections).

domainTags: removed 'interpretability' — none of the three source pages mention interpretability as a research priority for this call. The four stated research areas (sandboxes/testbeds, agent network science, agent infrastructure, oversight/control) map to evals, agent-security, and technical-alignment only.

funders.blurb: Microsoft Research's Accelerating Foundation Models Research (AFMR) Responsible AI track provided access to Azure-hosted foundation models and cloud resources to academics studying AI alignment, adversarial robustness, prompt injection defenses, and interpretability; the program has concluded and its successor is the Agentic AI Research and Innovation (AARI) initiative.
funders.thesis: "Align AI with shared human goals, values, and preferences via research on models which enhances safety, robustness, sustainability, responsibility, and transparency"

apply.notes: AFMR has concluded; pages are maintained for archival reference. Successor AARI program is not open for general applications — the current page shows only pre-selected project collaborations.

people.title: Senior Director, Microsoft Research (leads AARI successor program)

_verifyChanges: Blurb: Changed 'provided Azure compute grants to academics' to 'provided access to Azure-hosted foundation models and cloud resources to academics' — the fetched AFMR collaboration page states the program 'provides access to state-of-the-art foundation models hosted on Microsoft Azure through Microsoft Azure AI services,' not raw compute grants. The in-kind support is model API access, not bare compute; inKind=true is retained as accurate.

apply.notes: Removed the unsourced '2024-2025' year range from 'AFMR concluded in 2024-2025.' Neither the AFMR collaboration page nor the Responsible AI page specifies a conclusion year — the page states only 'The Accelerating Foundation Models Research program has reached its conclusion.'

funders.blurb: Amazon Web Services funds AI-security and agentic-AI research through the Amazon Research Awards (ARA) program — unrestricted grants averaging $70K–$80K plus AWS Promotional Credits to academic institutions — and committed $2.5M to the Linux Foundation's Alpha Omega initiative to help open-source maintainers manage AI-generated vulnerability reports.
funders.thesis: Awards are structured as unrestricted gifts to principal investigators' academic institutions, with no IP transferred to Amazon and recipients encouraged to publish openly and contribute to open-source repositories.

apply.notes: Spring 2026 cycle closed May 13, 2026; decisions expected August 2026. Fall cycle typically opens around October but no date has been published as of July 3, 2026. Separate rolling AWS Cloud Credit for Research program also available at aws.amazon.com/grants.

annualFieldGivingBasis.method: AWS committed $2.5M of a $12.5M joint investment with Anthropic, Google, Microsoft, and OpenAI into Alpha Omega (Linux Foundation) to help open-source maintainers validate and remediate AI-generated vulnerability reports, announced March 17, 2026.

_verifyChanges: apply.mode changed from 'rounds' to 'closed': the call-for-proposals page shows all Spring 2026 calls closed May 13, 2026, and no fall 2026 deadline is published as of July 3, 2026.
apply.notes updated: removed the present-tense framing of the spring cycle; noted fall cycle timing is typical but unconfirmed on the page.

thesis stripped of unverified quoted phrases: neither 'Advancing possible solutions for some of the most challenging problems in information security' nor 'advancing the frontiers of AI agents' appeared in the fetched page content; replaced with language supported by the ARA program pages.

blurb revised: 'agentic-AI-safety research' changed to 'agentic-AI research' because the Agentic AI call is primarily about advancing capabilities (healthcare, scientific discovery, software engineering), not safety-specific work.

domainTags: removed 'technical-alignment' (programs are cybersecurity and AI capabilities work, not AI alignment) and 'evals' (security red-teaming in these programs is not AI safety evaluation; tag is unsupported).
aliases: removed 'Amazon Responsible AI Grants' — this label did not appear on any fetched page and could not be verified.

grantees.blurb: MIRI pioneered technical AI alignment research (decision theory, agent foundations) and has since pivoted to policy advocacy focused on halting superintelligence development through internationally coordinated moratoriums.

CSET is a Georgetown University policy think tank that produces data-driven analysis on the national-security implications of AI — covering compute/semiconductor supply chains, US-China AI competition, and AI applications in defense — to inform policymakers on AI governance.

80,000 Hours is a nonprofit that builds the AI safety field by providing career research, one-on-one advising, and a job board to direct talented people into high-impact AI safety roles across technical research, policy, and operations.

RAND Corporation is a global nonprofit policy research organization whose AI-safety-relevant work (sized here by its AI governance and safety grants received) spans compute governance, catastrophic-risk policy, AI safety institute design, and tabletop exercises on AI-enabled threats.

UC Berkeley hosts CHAI (Center for Human-Compatible AI, founded by Stuart Russell) and BAIR, conducting foundational AI safety research in value alignment, interpretability, and robustness (sized here by its AI-safety-relevant grants received).

MIT (sized here by its AI-safety-relevant grants received) hosts the CSAIL Algorithmic Alignment Group on technical alignment, the MIT AI Risk Initiative maintaining a 1,700+ risk taxonomy used by the UN and EU AI Office, and the AI Agent Index evaluating safety transparency in deployed agents.

BERI provides operational, financial, and administrative support (HR, grant management, compliance) to university AI-safety and x-risk research groups such as CHAI, FHI, and SERI, enabling them to focus on research rather than bureaucracy.

Lightcone Infrastructure builds and maintains LessWrong and the AI Alignment Forum — the primary online platforms for AI safety and rationalist research discussion — alongside the Lighthaven event venue in Berkeley, providing core community infrastructure for the field.

Epoch AI is a research nonprofit that tracks AI training compute, model capabilities, and hardware investment trends to forecast transformative AI timelines and inform AI safety strategy for grantmakers and policymakers.

BlueDot Impact is a non-profit talent accelerator that runs AI safety courses and career-transition programs to build the pipeline of professionals working on beneficial AI, having trained 7,000+ people at labs, government agencies, and AI safety organizations since 2022.

Stanford University conducts AI safety research through the Stanford Center for AI Safety and Stanford HAI, spanning technical alignment, trustworthy AI systems, and policy/governance — sized here by its AI-safety-relevant grants received.

GovAI is an independent nonprofit that conducts rigorous research on AI policy, threat modeling, and institutional risk management to help governments and decision-makers navigate the transition to advanced AI.

OpenMined is a nonprofit building open-source privacy-preserving AI infrastructure (PySyft/Syft) for federated computation across private datasets without centralizing data, with AI-safety-relevant work on privacy-preserving model evaluations (NIST partnership) and AI governance policy (UK Frontier AI Taskforce, U.S. Senate AI roadmap).

CNAS is a nonpartisan national security think tank (sized here by its AI-safety-relevant policy work) that researches AI governance frameworks, international stability risks from advanced AI, and federal model assessment standards for the DoD and intelligence community.

AI Safety Support provided field-building services — coaching, community networking, and career resources — to early-career and independent researchers entering the AI safety ecosystem, before pausing operations indefinitely in July 2023 due to funding exhaustion and founder burnout.

ESPR is a free 10-day residential camp in the UK for mathematically talented 16–19 year olds that teaches rationality, AI safety, and AI evaluation to build the pipeline of future alignment-aware researchers.
